scenes.title: Program Welcome and Purpose
scenes.objective: Set why this training matters and what successful completion requires.
scenes.script: Open by framing operator safety outcomes, then connect training participation to real incident prevention.
steps: Welcome learners and confirm role-specific attendance.
State the training scope for Classes 1-5 and Class 7 awareness.
Set participation expectations for discussion, written checks, and practical demonstration.
Reinforce stop-work authority and no-shortcut culture.
ask: What jobsite habit most often turns into forklift near-misses in your area?
watchFor: Language that normalizes rushed operation or bypassing checks.

items: Most serious events involve speed, blind travel, or weak communication.
Incidents are usually process failures, not one-time bad luck.
Clear expectations early reduce unsafe improvisation later.

scenes.notes: Keep this opening high-energy and practical, not compliance-heavy.

scenes.title: Trainer and Operator Responsibilities
scenes.objective: Clarify trainer qualification requirements and operator obligations.
scenes.script: Walk through qualified-person expectations, then tie operator accountability to daily decisions.
steps: Define trainer qualification using ANSI/OSHA language in plain terms.
Confirm written exam and practical proficiency expectations.
Review operator duty to report hazards and defects promptly.
Assign accountability checkpoints for supervision and sign-off.
ask: What is the most common responsibility gap between trainer intent and operator behavior?
watchFor: Participants viewing certification as paperwork instead of competency.

items: Qualification must be demonstrated, not assumed from tenure.
Operator responsibility includes active hazard identification.
Recordkeeping validates competency and recurring evaluation.

scenes.notes: Use real examples of past training misses to make the standards concrete.

scenes.title: Course Preparation and Areas of Focus
scenes.objective: Prepare instructors to set up environment, materials, and agenda for effective delivery.
scenes.script: Review prep requirements, then walk through focus areas that drive operator performance.
steps: Confirm classroom and practical area readiness before start.
Validate equipment condition and training aid availability.
Sequence agenda around highest-risk operations first.
Set checkpoints for knowledge verification and coaching.
ask: Which part of course setup most often fails in your current process?
watchFor: Overloaded agendas that reduce practice quality.

items: Preparation quality directly predicts hands-on safety performance.
Practice design must reflect real workplace hazards.
Evaluation standards should be visible before practical exercises.

scenes.notes: Keep this scene operational and checklist-driven.

scenes.title: Regulatory Landscape: OSHA, ANSI, CSA
scenes.objective: Differentiate regulatory obligations and where each standard applies.
scenes.script: Map OSHA requirements to ANSI/ITSDF and CSA references without overwhelming learners.
steps: Explain OSHA 1910.178 operator training rule baseline.
Distinguish ANSI/ITSDF B56 family applicability by truck class.
Clarify when CSA references are used in your operating context.
Tie standards to daily operator decisions and supervision.
ask: Where do teams most often confuse guideline versus requirement?
watchFor: Participants treating standards as optional policy guidance.

items: OSHA establishes enforceable baseline requirements.
ANSI/ITSDF provides detailed equipment and operation standards.
Training records and evaluation are mandatory, not optional.

scenes.notes: Use local site examples to avoid abstract standards talk.

scenes.title: Capacity and Stability Rules
scenes.objective: Apply capacity and stability concepts to both forklifts and telehandlers.
scenes.script: Contrast data plate limits with load chart variability and reinforce no-estimation behavior.
steps: Review forklift data plate interpretation and load center impact.
Review telehandler load chart variables: boom angle and extension.
Model capacity reduction with attachments and rigging included.
Run one scenario where overconfidence creates instability.
ask: What information do you verify before every pick, regardless of schedule pressure?
watchFor: Estimating weight or relying on memory over plate/chart verification.

items: Never exceed rated capacity shown on plate/chart.
Load center changes can invalidate nominal capacity.
Attachment weight counts against available lifting capacity.

scenes.notes: Use quick math checks and challenge learners to justify each pick.

scenes.title: Safe Use Plan and Evaluation Standards
scenes.objective: Build a site-specific safe use plan and evaluation process.
scenes.script: Translate standards into a practical plan: route design, controls, and measurable competency checks.
steps: Identify site hazards, traffic conflicts, and pedestrian interfaces.
Define route rules, speed controls, and communication points.
Document written and practical evaluation criteria.
Set re-evaluation cadence and trigger events.
ask: What single change would make your current safe use plan more enforceable?
watchFor: Plans that list policy but omit observable behavior criteria.

items: Safe use plans must be task and site specific.
Evaluation standards should be observable and repeatable.
Re-evaluation triggers include incidents, near misses, and equipment changes.

scenes.notes: This scene should output one actionable revision to current site plans.

sections.title: Section 3: Five Principles of Safe Operation

scenes.title: Five Principles Overview
scenes.objective: Anchor all practical behaviors to the five principles framework.
scenes.script: Introduce each principle and connect it to one frequent failure mode observed in the field.
steps: Introduce: avoid hazards, pre-op inspect, function test, workplace inspect, intended use.
Map each principle to one incident type.
Explain how principles apply across classes with equipment differences.
Set expectation that every later demo calls back to one principle.
ask: Which principle is most often skipped under production pressure?
watchFor: Participants remembering steps but not underlying principle intent.

items: The five principles are behavior anchors, not poster slogans.
Skipping pre-op and workplace checks drives preventable incidents.
Intended-use violations often begin with schedule pressure.

scenes.notes: Make this fast and memorable; use one-line examples per principle.

scenes.title: Awareness and Positioning
scenes.objective: Improve situational awareness and positioning decisions for mixed environments.
scenes.script: Demonstrate 360-degree scan habits, blind-corner communication, and positioning choices before movement.
steps: Run pre-move scan and identify dynamic hazards.
Compare forklift tail swing and telehandler boom blind spots.
Practice stop-horn-scan decision at intersections.
Apply positioning checks before pickup and placement.
ask: When visibility is compromised, what must happen before movement continues?
watchFor: Turning behavior before complete hazard scan.

items: If vision is blocked, stop and use spotter/procedure before moving.
Forklift tail swing and telehandler boom both create non-obvious strike zones.
Positioning is a pre-move decision, not a mid-turn correction.

scenes.notes: Use cones or taped zones if available for spatial learning.

scenes.title: Inspection and Function Testing Discipline
scenes.objective: Standardize pre-op and function test behavior before operation begins.
scenes.script: Move from checklist recitation to evidence-based inspection and test execution.
steps: Review pre-op inspection sequence and defect thresholds.
Perform function tests for steering, brakes, hydraulics, and warning devices.
Document deficiencies and tag out when criteria are met.
Escalate defects with clear chain-of-communication.
ask: What defect would you escalate immediately even if production is behind?
watchFor: Informal pass/fail decisions without documentation evidence.

items: Braking, steering, and hydraulic failures are immediate no-go.
Function tests confirm operability beyond visual inspection.
Documentation is part of safety, not administrative overhead.

scenes.notes: Require one learner to narrate each check step aloud during demo.

scenes.title: Forklift vs Telehandler Operational Differences
scenes.objective: Compare handling behavior so operators choose correct controls and pacing.
scenes.script: Contrast steering, center-of-gravity behavior, and surface demands with practical examples.
steps: Review rear-wheel steering implications for forklifts.
Review dynamic center-of-gravity shifts for telehandlers.
Compare stopping distances and speed management expectations.
Identify environment-specific risks: indoor aisles vs rough terrain.
ask: Which operational difference causes the most transfer errors when operators switch equipment?
watchFor: Participants applying passenger-vehicle instincts to lift equipment.

items: Steering and stability response is fundamentally different from cars.
Telehandler boom movement changes capacity and stability continuously.
Surface conditions must be evaluated before movement, not after.

scenes.notes: Use this as the bridge from classroom to practical field drills.

scenes.title: Visibility, Travel, and Spotter Protocol
scenes.objective: Drive consistent travel behavior when vision is limited.
scenes.script: Set non-negotiable travel rules: low load travel, reverse when blocked, and spotter coordination.
steps: Demonstrate travel posture and mast/boom positioning for visibility.
Trigger horn and communication protocol at blind points.
Decide when to stop and assign spotter support.
Validate clear route before resuming travel.
ask: What exact conditions require a spotter in your operation?
watchFor: Moving with partial visibility instead of resetting position.

items: Blocked visibility requires stop and alternate procedure before moving.
Spotter communication must be explicit and acknowledged.
Travel speed is always subordinate to visibility and route certainty.

scenes.notes: Run one timed drill emphasizing decision quality over speed.

scenes.title: Machine Modification and Attachment Controls
scenes.objective: Prevent unsafe modifications and attachment misuse.
scenes.script: Reinforce that unauthorized modifications invalidate safe operation assumptions and capacity references.
steps: Explain written approval requirements for modifications.
Review attachment compatibility and plate/chart updates.
Flag liability and warranty impact of unauthorized changes.
Set escalation path for suspected non-compliant equipment.
ask: How do you verify an attachment is approved before use?
watchFor: Assuming 'it fits' means it is approved and safe.

items: Modifications require written manufacturer approval.
Attachments change capacity and require updated references.
No operator should use equipment with uncertain compliance status.

scenes.notes: Close by summarizing enforceable do-not-operate triggers.
